title: Designing low energy capture transfers for spacecraft to the Moon and Mars
speaker: Edward Belbruno
affiliation: Princeton University and Innovative Orbital Design, Inc.
abstract: In 1991 a new type of transfer to the Moon was operationally demonstrated by the Japanese spacecraft, Hiten, using ballistic capture. It was designed by this speaker and James Miller. This is capture about the Moon which is automatic so that no rocket engines are required. It was accomplished due to the existence of regions in phase space called weak stability boundaries, where ballistic capture occurs. These are complex fractal regions of unstable chaotic motion. Until recently it was thought that such a transfer to Mars was not feasible.

proof_stages.label: Hohmann-transfer benchmark and delta-v metric
proof_stages.summary: The talk first formulates standard patched two-body Hohmann transfers and identifies the departure, plane-change, and especially capture delta-v/V-infinity as the benchmark cost and risk to beat.

proof_stages.label: Ballistic capture and weak-stability boundary
proof_stages.summary: Ballistic capture is defined through negative Kepler energy relative to the target body, and the weak-stability-boundary algorithm is introduced as the numerical transition set between n-cycle stable motion and escape.

proof_stages.label: Lunar low-energy-transfer mechanism
proof_stages.summary: Earlier lunar work shows how starting from the lunar weak-stability boundary, using backward-time/exterior transfers, and interpreting the region via invariant manifolds yields practical zero-capture-delta-v transfers.

proof_stages.label: Mars direct-capture obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The lunar mechanism appears not to transfer directly to Mars because Hohmann arrivals have roughly 3 km/s hyperbolic excess while Mars moves about 25 km/s around the Sun, making weak capture by direct arrival implausible and outer-planet detours impractically slow.

proof_stages.label: Remote weak-capture-point strategy for Mars
proof_stages.summary: The central design idea is to aim not at Mars itself but at remote weak-capture points XC along or near Mars's orbit, match the required velocity there with a delta-v, and then let the Sun-Mars dynamics carry the spacecraft into ballistic capture after prescribed cycles.

proof_stages.label: Numerical construction of Mars capture sets and transfers
proof_stages.summary: Within a planar elliptic restricted three-body model, the computation finds Mars stable sets with n forward cycles and immediate backward escape, back-propagates them to global arcs of admissible XC points, and optimizes Earth-to-XC transfers against a velocity cost.

proof_stages.label: Delta-v savings and operational consequences
proof_stages.summary: The computed Mars transfers are displayed and compared with the four Hohmann classes, showing substantial capture-delta-v savings in selected cases, more flexible launch windows, and a less time-critical capture process.

examples.name: Lunar Getaway Special ballistic-capture study
examples.note: The 1986 two-and-a-half-year lunar trajectory demonstrated zero-capture-Δv motion and is presented as what opened the low-energy-transfer idea, while also motivating the search for shorter practical versions.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker traces the Lunar Getaway Special trajectory, its two-year spiral, ballistic leg, weak-stability-boundary capture, 3D behavior, and KAM-torus obstruction

passes.reason: A zero-delta-v lunar ballistic-capture trajectory lies on the capture/escape boundary, but is used as a positive proof of concept.

examples.name: Hiten Japanese lunar rescue
examples.note: The failed Japanese lunar mission is used to illustrate that the exterior-transfer method could rescue a real spacecraft and make the technique visible to practitioners.
examples.engagement_reason: Hiten is invoked as a failed Japanese mission rescued by applying the method, without working through its trajectory or computations

passes.reason: The Hiten rescue used the low-energy exterior/ballistic-capture mechanism to reach the Moon, serving as a successful demonstration.

examples.name: Hypothetical Jupiter-assisted Mars capture
examples.note: The imagined route using Jupiter's weak-stability region would take on the order of a hundred years, so it visibly motivates abandoning direct/outer-planet capture ideas for the remote-XC Mars strategy.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker sketches the Jupiter-assisted Mars-capture scenario and does a feasibility check, including how Jupiter would slow the craft and the rough hundred-year flight-time estimate

passes.reason: The Jupiter-assisted Mars-capture idea is presented as a would-be construction whose roughly hundred-year flight time makes it impractical.
The Jupiter-assisted Mars route is a hypothetical weak-boundary ballistic-capture workaround, but is deployed to show that this construction is impractically slow.
the Jupiter-assisted Mars-capture idea is presented as an impractically slow attempted workaround showing that route would not work

examples.name: Mars Observer capture-burn failure
examples.note: In response to a risk question, the Mars Observer failure illustrates why replacing a fast periapsis capture burn by a remote, non-time-critical maneuver is operationally valuable.
examples.engagement_reason: Mars Observer is cited only as an example of a risky capture-burn failure
